texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we report on the design, construction and optimization of a pilot scale 3-stage membrane plant operated by a single compressor for production of high-purity CH4 and CO2 from crude biogas. In our previous works, we developed asymmetric polysulfone hollow fiber membranes with high CO2/CH4 separation properties and a numerical simulation program for a 3-stage membrane process for biogas separation. Herein, utilizing a pure gas permeation test, we report the effects of feed pressure and temperature on gas separation properties of the developed membrane modules. We also report the effects of various operating conditions on CH4 purity and recovery at the retentate, which were investigated using mixed gases as a function of stage-cut. Using a simulation program and the gas permeation data, we designed and constructed a 3-stage membrane plant that can treat 100 Nm 3 /h of crude biogas (65-75 vol% CH4, balanced CO2). It was confirmed that, in the constructed 3-stage pilot plant, the final purity and recovery ratios for CH4 and CO2 of the 3stage membrane plant were significantly influenced by the operation conditions. Under optimized operation conditions, the 3-stage membrane plant exhibited outstanding biogas separation performance; high CH4 purity and good recovery (98.4% and 98.7%, respectively) were observed, together with high CO2 purity and recovery (97.2% and 97.0%, respectively). This study demonstrates the commercial applicability of the 3-stage membrane pilot plant using polysulfone hollow fiber membranes for simultaneous production of high purity CH4 and CO2 from crude biogas.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the in 21st century, problems of fossil fuel shortage and global warming have become more serious. CO2 is well-known as the primary greenhouse gas contributing to global warming, but CH4 has a 210times larger global warming potential than that of CO2 and represents the second largest contribution to global warming. A huge amount of crude biogas (mainly CH4 and CO2) has been emitted in the treatment of food and animal wastes at landfill sites, etc. Therefore, biogas separation or upgrading has drawn growing interest in terms of production of renewable natural gas and reduction of greenhouse gas emissions [1-5]. If high purity CH4 can be separated from biogas, it can be used as a renewable source of natural gas for automobile fuel and city gas. If high purity CO2 can be rejected and separated, it can also be valuably used in carbon capture, sequestration and carbon utilization. Accordingly, many researchers are intensively engaged in the development of suitable technologies for biogas production, purification and utilization [6-12].
texts.text: In the in 21st century, problems of fossil fuel shortage and global warming have become more serious. CO2 is well-known as the primary greenhouse gas contributing to global warming, but CH4 has a 210times larger global warming potential than that of CO2 and represents the second largest contribution to global warming. A huge amount of crude biogas (mainly CH4 and CO2) has been emitted in the treatment of food and animal wastes at landfill sites, etc. Therefore, biogas separation or upgrading has drawn growing interest in terms of production of renewable natural gas and reduction of greenhouse gas emissions [1-5]. If high purity CH4 can be separated from biogas, it can be used as a renewable source of natural gas for automobile fuel and city gas. If high purity CO2 can be rejected and separated, it can also be valuably used in carbon capture, sequestration and carbon utilization. Accordingly, many researchers are intensively engaged in the development of suitable technologies for biogas production, purification and utilization [6-12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Crude biogas mixture is produced or emitted at anaerobic digestion sites and typically contains CH4 (60-70 vol%), CO2 (30-40 vol%) and trace impurities of H2S, NH3, siloxane, etc. After removing the trace impurities by desulfurization and deammonium technologies, etc., several biogas upgrading technologies have been used to remove CO2 from CH4 mixtures, including chemical scrubbing, water scrubbing, pressure swing adsorption (PSA) and membrane processes [13-20]. Among them, membrane technology has been considered very suitable for biogas upgrading [21-29] because it has some practical advantages, including environmental-friendly process without production of harmful waste (e.g., amine absorbents), easy operation, low energy cost, cheap plant cost and so on. Through a comparison study with 250 Nm 3 /h commercial biogas upgrading plants, a BCC research report on the technical market also suggested that membrane separation is more promising than cryogenic or other competing technologies in terms of plant cost etc. [19,30].

Among them, membrane technology has been considered very suitable for biogas upgrading [21-29] because it has some practical advantages, including environmental-friendly process without production of harmful waste (e.g., amine absorbents), easy operation, low energy cost, cheap plant cost and so on. Through a comparison study with 250 Nm 3 /h commercial biogas upgrading plants, a BCC research report on the technical market also suggested that membrane separation is more promising than cryogenic or other competing technologies in terms of plant cost etc. [19,30].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In terms of commercial-scale biogas separation, primary membranebased biogas upgrading facilities are reported to use a one- or twostage membrane process. A one-stage membrane plant was launched in the 1990s in Collendoorn (the Netherlands), and obtained 88% pure CH4 at a flow rate of 25 m 3 (STP)/h; this gas was supplied it to a city gas pipeline [8]. In 1993 in Los Angeles County in the US, a two stage membrane plant with recirculation using UOP (Separex™) membranes was launched on a scale of 2600 m 3 /h, and produced 97.5% pure CH4 [27]. Recently, Evonik Co. in Germany introduced a commercial-scale 3-stage membrane process with recirculation to produce high purity CH4 (>98%) using highly methane-selective P84 ® polyimide based hollow fiber membranes (Tradename: Sepuran™) [31]. According to Evonik's technical report, the company has installed and successfully operated many biogas purification plants with around 1000 Nm 3 /h in the world.
texts.text: In terms of commercial-scale biogas separation, primary membranebased biogas upgrading facilities are reported to use a one- or twostage membrane process. A one-stage membrane plant was launched in the 1990s in Collendoorn (the Netherlands), and obtained 88% pure CH4 at a flow rate of 25 m 3 (STP)/h; this gas was supplied it to a city gas pipeline [8]. In 1993 in Los Angeles County in the US, a two stage membrane plant with recirculation using UOP (Separex™) membranes was launched on a scale of 2600 m 3 /h, and produced 97.5% pure CH4 [27]. Recently, Evonik Co. in Germany introduced a commercial-scale 3-stage membrane process with recirculation to produce high purity CH4 (>98%) using highly methane-selective P84 ® polyimide based hollow fiber membranes (Tradename: Sepuran™) [31]. According to Evonik's technical report, the company has installed and successfully operated many biogas purification plants with around 1000 Nm 3 /h in the world.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From an academic point of view, the separation and recovery performance of the membrane process are mainly governed by the separation efficiency of the membranes and the configuration design of the multi-stage membrane process. The separation efficiency is primarily influenced by the selectivity of the membrane materials and permeation flux of the composite membranes and therefore, some researchers on biogas separation have focused on developing new membrane materials and composite membranes with better permeability and separation properties for CO2/CH4 [32-37]. The separation performance of the membrane process is also governed by the process configuration, including the number of membrane stages, the membrane area of each stage, the upstream and downstream pressures and the connection of each stage with/without compressor or vacuum pump [38]. However, optimization of the process configuration becomes more difficult and complex as the number of separation stages increases, resulting in the need for a simulation-based approach for process design or, sometimes, leading to evaluation of a limited number of process configurations with limited operation conditions for selected membranes.

The separation efficiency is primarily influenced by the selectivity of the membrane materials and permeation flux of the composite membranes and therefore, some researchers on biogas separation have focused on developing new membrane materials and composite membranes with better permeability and separation properties for CO2/CH4 [32-37]. However, optimization of the process configuration becomes more difficult and complex as the number of separation stages increases, resulting in the need for a simulation-based approach for process design or, sometimes, leading to evaluation of a limited number of process configurations with limited operation conditions for selected membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many previous studies have reported on biogas upgrading processes using multi-stage membranes with composite or asymmetric membranes to obtain high CH4 purity and high recovery [13,14,17,25-27,39-42]. Ismail et al. developed asymmetric polysulfone hollow fiber membranes via a dry/wet phase inversion process, installed them in a bench scale plant in series and cascade module configurations and studied the CO2/CH4 separation behavior [40]. They suggested that the three-stage cascade membrane configuration could produce high CO2/CH4 selectivity at low operation pressure and two stage-cut conditions. Our previous studies reported the use of an asymmetric polysulfone membrane module in a 2-stage recirculation membrane process for purification of high purity (> 95%) bio-methane that can be applied to vehicle fuel [41]. We also developed a numerical analysis method for the separation of CO2 and CH4 using a 2-stage membrane process [42]. By adjusting the ratio of membrane area (1:1, 1:3, 2:2) in the first and second membrane modules, the feed rate and CH4 recovery at 95% CH4 purity increased from 47.1 to 92.5%, respectively. Our 2-stage membrane process using recycled flows can provide both CH4 concentration richer than 95% to meet general vehicle fuel standards and recovery higher than 90%. M. Scholz et al. investigated membrane materials and module configurations, but their reported simulation results comparing 2- or 3-stage membrane processes did not include experiments of pilot-scale plant operation [25]. Oi et al. [43] also designed 2- and 3-stage membrane processes with different configurations and suggested an efficient methodology to optimize the product compositions and to estimate the membrane area in the multi-stage process by using their approximate permeator model and mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP).

Our 2-stage membrane process using recycled flows can provide both CH4 concentration richer than 95% to meet general vehicle fuel standards and recovery higher than 90%. M. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In spite of many intensive studies on biogas separation, basic studies on the effects of feed pressure and temperature on the pure gas permeation flux and separation properties of CH4 and CO2, respectively, and even the effect of pressure, temperature and compositions on the purity and recovery of CH4 in retentate and CO2 in permeate side as a function of stage-cut (or the ratio of permeate flux to feed flux) have not been undertaken systematically. To the best of our knowledge, meaningful studies on the effects of operating pressure and temperature, membrane area ratio of each stage and total membrane area of the 3stage membrane process on the final purity and recovery of CH4 and CO2 in pilot-scale 3-stage membrane process using large-sized membrane modules with high CO2/CH4 separation performance in terms of biogas upgrading have until now not been reported.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Herein, we adopted an asymmetric polysulfone (PSf) hollow fiber (HF) membrane module with high permeation flux and high CO2/CH4 separation properties for biogas upgrading; module was developed using dry-wet spinning techniques in our previous work [44]. Based on the obtained permeation data, a numerical simulation program was adopted that was developed using Visual Fortran in our previous works [42,45,46]. To design and install the pilot-scale membrane process, 2inch and 4-inch HF membrane modules were developed. Using the 2inch membrane modules, we investigated the permeation flux and separation properties of hollow fiber membranes developed by changing the feed pressure (2~ 10 bar) and temperature (15~ 35 °C) of the pure and mixed gases. For the mixed gases, the effects of feed pressures, temperatures and compositions on the CH4 purity and recovery ratio in retentate were investigated as a function of stage-cut (0.1 ~ 0.9). texts.orig: 2.1. Gas separation properties of PSf HF membrane modules
texts.text: 2.1. Gas separation properties of PSf HF membrane modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Asymmetric polysulfone HF membranes and 2 in. modules were developed previously using typical dry-wet spinning techniques in cooperation with Synopex Inc. and KRICT, Korea [44]. Herein, 18 wt% PSf in NMP/1,4-Dioxane (72/10 wt%) solution was prepared and small amounts of primary and secondary alcohols were added to the dope solution to increase the gas permeation flux by introducing sponge structure to the hollow fiber. After degassing, the dope solution at 80 °C was extruded through a tube-in-orifice type spinneret at a short air gap of 30 cm. The spun fibers were first rinsed in a flowing water bath at room temperature for at least 24 h; they were then immersed in methanol and, subsequently, n -hexane to allow complete removal of any residual solvent in the fibers. Finally, the washed fibers were dried in air for over 24 h. The dried PES hollow fibers were dip-coated in 2 wt% PDMS (Sylgard 184) solutions in n -hexane to plug the defects on the membrane outer surfaces. The fiber morphology was reanalyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-840A). SEM images of the developed hollow fiber membranes are shown in Fig. 1. The SEM images show that the hollow fiber has an asymmetric porous sponge like structure, with some macrovoids in cross-section (a, b) and a dense inner surface (c) and a porous outer surface (d). 2-inch hollow fiber modules were prepared using polysulfone hollow fiber membranes and used for lab-scale pure and mixed gas separation tests. Large sized 4inch modules were developed for this study and used for the 3-stage membrane plant, which had 100 Nm 3 /h of operating capacity. Detailed module specifications are summarized in Table 1.

texts.orig: In our previous study, a computer simulation program with Visual Fortran Numerical simulation of a three-stage membrane process incorporating a recycling system with one compressor was performed to meet the compressed natural gas (CNG) fuel standards in Korea (CH4 > 95-97%). Detailed descriptions of the computer program for the 2- and 3-stage membrane processes are available in our previous studies [45,46].
texts.text: In our previous study, a computer simulation program with Visual Fortran Numerical simulation of a three-stage membrane process incorporating a recycling system with one compressor was performed to meet the compressed natural gas (CNG) fuel standards in Korea (CH4 > 95-97%). Detailed descriptions of the computer program for the 2- and 3-stage membrane processes are available in our previous studies [45,46].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on our previous studies, numerical simulation of the 3-stage membrane separation process with recirculation at one compressor was conducted in this study by using the pure and mixed gas permeance data obtained with 2-inch HF modules; a schematic diagram of the three-stage membrane process is shown in Fig. 3. Membrane process was designed to achieve > 95-99% CH4 purity at recovery of > 95-99% CH4 to meet standards for Korean biogas purification for both transport fuel (> 95%) and grid injection (> 97%) in retentate or upstream in the 2 nd stage. Balanced 95-99% CO2 purity at > 95-99% recovery was observed in the permeate or downstream in the 3 rd membrane stage.
texts.text: Based on our previous studies, numerical simulation of the 3-stage membrane separation process with recirculation at one compressor was conducted in this study by using the pure and mixed gas permeance data obtained with 2-inch HF modules; a schematic diagram of the three-stage membrane process is shown in Fig. 3. Membrane process was designed to achieve > 95-99% CH4 purity at recovery of > 95-99% CH4 to meet standards for Korean biogas purification for both transport fuel (> 95%) and grid injection (> 97%) in retentate or upstream in the 2 nd stage. Balanced 95-99% CO2 purity at > 95-99% recovery was observed in the permeate or downstream in the 3 rd membrane stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to the simulation results, the proposed simulation conditions for a fixed feed gas composition of 65-75 vol% CH4/25-35 vol% CO2 are max.10 bar upstream pressure (in consideration of Korean safety regulations for biogas equipment installation) and min.0 bar downstream pressure; 15 to 35 °C of operating temperature; and membrane area ratios of 1 st :2 nd :3 rd =1:1-5:1. The total membrane area required to treat 100 Nm 3 /h of mixed gas was approximately 400-600 m 2 relying on operation conditions, which include bore side feed to decrease the concentration polarization in the feed mixture and countercurrent flow mode with 10 bar of operating upstream pressure and 0 bar of downstream pressure; another important factor is the membrane area of each stage of the 3-stage membrane process. A total membrane area of 1200 m 2 (twice ~ three times as large as in the simulation results), obtained by installing 4-inch modules in parallel in each stage, was installed to experimentally determine the optimal operating conditions for securing design safety of pilot plant.

texts.orig: The pilot-scale membrane plant was composed of various pretreatment equipment in series, including wet-pretreatment, primary dehumidification, compressor, secondary dehumidification, siloxane removal and cooling before installed membrane module equipment (Fig. 4). Wet-pretreatment was carried out using the desulfurizationammonia removal techniques developed by ENV (Chung-Hae ENV co., LTD, Korea); these methods were found to remove H2S and NH3 to below 1 ppm. A two-stage dehumidifying unit comprised of cooling and desiccant dryers (dew point temperature of -50 °C) was constructed to protect the membrane module from moisture, which would otherwise deteriorate its exact separation and purification performance. Biogas was compressed to 10 bar with a screw-type compressor (oil-type, max. compressing capacity of 300 m 3 /hr), and then a siloxane absorption tower was used to remove the siloxane to the extent that it was undetectable. Before flowing into the membrane separation unit, the biogas was cooled to 5-25 °C in a cooling unit.
texts.text: The pilot-scale membrane plant was composed of various pretreatment equipment in series, including wet-pretreatment, primary dehumidification, compressor, secondary dehumidification, siloxane removal and cooling before installed membrane module equipment (Fig. 4). Wet-pretreatment was carried out using the desulfurizationammonia removal techniques developed by ENV (Chung-Hae ENV co., LTD, Korea); these methods were found to remove H2S and NH3 to below 1 ppm. A two-stage dehumidifying unit comprised of cooling and desiccant dryers (dew point temperature of -50 °C) was constructed to protect the membrane module from moisture, which would otherwise deteriorate its exact separation and purification performance. Biogas was compressed to 10 bar with a screw-type compressor (oil-type, max. compressing capacity of 300 m 3 /hr), and then a siloxane absorption tower was used to remove the siloxane to the extent that it was undetectable. Before flowing into the membrane separation unit, the biogas was cooled to 5-25 °C in a cooling unit.

The pressure normalized pure gas permeation flux or permeance and separation properties of the 2-inch hollow fiber membrane modules were measured and the obtained results for CO2/CH4 selectivity and CO2 permeation flux are listed in Table 3. For comparison, previous studies with polysulfone membranes results for CO2/CH4 selectivity and CO2 permeation flux are listed in Table 3. Our developed membranes showed approximate CO2 permeability of 103.4 GPU and CO2/CH4 selectivity of 39.3. texts.orig: We also investigated the effect of feed temperature (15-35 °C at 10 bar) and pressure (2-10 bar at 25° C) changes on gas permeation flux and separation factor of the developed 2-inch membrane modules (membrane area: 0.95 m 2 ). The obtained results are shown in Fig. 5. It can be observed that the CO2 permeance is much higher than the CH4 permeance; this is explained by the fact that CO2 has higher critical temperature (CO2: 31.2 °C and CH4: -82.6 °C) and smaller kinetic diameter (CO2: 3.30 Å and CH4: 3.80 Å) and has higher solubility and diffusivity than those of CH4 in polysulfone membranes.

texts.orig: The effects of pressure of mixed gas across the membranes on CH4 purity and recovery on the retentate side were investigated using a 2inch HF membrane as a function of stage cut (i.e., ratio of permeate to feed flow rate: 0.1-0.8) with 70 vol% CH4/30 vol% CO2 feed concentration. The pressure was controlled in a range from 2 to 10 bar. As can be seen in Fig. 6, CH4 purity increased and CH4 recovery decreased with increasing stage-cut. Similar results were observed in the previous studies [54,55] and can be explained as follows: increasing the stagecut means an increase in the residence time or contacting time of gases. At low stage-cut of about 0.2, the more permeable CO2 permeate is dominant, the concentration of residual CH4 highly increases and the concentration of residual CO2 decreases. But with increase of stage-cut from 0.2 to 0.8, the residual CH4 also permeates more, the CH4 purity increases and the recovery rate decreases. In Fig. 6(b), the recovery of CH4 in the retentate was plotted with increase of feed pressure from 2 to 10 bar. A 95% CH4 purity are observed at 75.3% and 27.9%, at feed pressures of 6 and 10 bar, at stage cuts of 0.64 and 0.45, respectively. This indicates that increasing pressure can highly enhance CH4 purity and recovery ratio. Similar results were observed in previous studies [46,56,57]. This effect can be reasonably explained as based on pure gas permeation and by selectivity performance against feed pressure, as shown in Fig. 5(a); at higher pressure, CO2 permeation decreases while CH4 permeation increases; this can highly increase the CO2/CH4 selectivity. The optimum stage cut to achieve > 95% CH4 purity in retentate are observed to be 0.45 at 10 bar and 0.64 at 6 bar, which indicate that increased feed pressure is favorable for bio gas separation in the membrane process.

texts.orig: The feed compositions of the feed gas at feed temperature of 25 °C and feed pressure of 10 bar changed from 30 vol% CH4/70 vol% CO2 to 90 vol% CH4/10 vol% CO2; the obtained CH4 purity and recovery ratio at the retentate side as a function of stage cut (0.1-0.9) are shown in Fig. 8. When the CH4 concentration increased from to 30% to 90% in the mixed feed gas, the CH4 purity in retentate improved from 59.0% to 97.7% at the stage-cut of 0.5. The stage cuts required to achieve > 95.0% CH4 purity are calculated to be 0.50 and 0.22, with corresponding recoveries of 69.6 and 82.0%, respectively. This indicates that it is difficult to obtain CH4 with high purity of > 95% and high recovery of > 95-99% in a single-stage membrane process, indicating that a multi-stage membrane system should be used. From the mixed gas experimental results shown in Figs. 6, 7 and 8, it can be seen that CH4 purity or concentration and CH4 recovery in retentate are highly influenced by feed temperature, pressure and feed compositions; therefore, optimum feed pressure and temperature should be carefully determined in consideration of environmental conditions of installation sites.

From the mixed gas experimental results shown in Figs. 6, 7 and 8, it can be seen that CH4 purity or concentration and CH4 recovery in retentate are highly influenced by feed temperature, pressure and feed compositions; therefore, optimum feed pressure and temperature should be carefully determined in consideration of environmental conditions of installation sites.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Construction and operation of 100 Nm 3 /hr pilot plant 3-stage recirculation membrane process
texts.text: 3.3. Construction and operation of 100 Nm 3 /hr pilot plant 3-stage recirculation membrane process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the simulation scheme in Fig. 3 with our previous simulation program, we designed a pilot-scale 3-stage biogas upgrading membrane process with recirculation. We used one compressor to produce high purity CH4 and CO2 with a 100 Nm 3 /h biogas mixture. The constructed pilot plant and details of equipment are shown in Fig. 9. To optimize the 3-stage membrane process and achieve 95-99% CH4 purity with 95-99% CH4 recovery and balanced high recovery and purity of CO2, the feed gas composition was in the range of 70.5-74.2 vol% CH4. The operating pressures varied from 5 to 10 bar for max. upstream pressure and stayed at 0 bar of min. downstream pressure. Operating temperature was controlled in a range from 5 to 25 °C. About forty 4-inch hollow fiber membrane (membrane area: 40 m 2 ) were installed in parallel in each stage of the pilot membrane plant.

texts.orig: 3.3.1. Effects of operating pressure and temperature on CH4 purity and recovery
texts.text: 3.3.1. Effects of operating pressure and temperature on CH4 purity and recovery

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using the pilot plant with 4-inch membrane modules (membrane area: 40 m 2 ), systematic study was conducted by adjusting operation conditions (feed pressure and temperature) to obtain high CH4 purity and recovery in a 3-stage membrane plant. Fig. 10(a) shows the results obtained at feed pressures of 7, 8, 9 and 10 bar. With increasing operation pressure, the CH4 purity increased from 93.7 to 98.8% and the recovery ratio increased from 94.6 to 99.1%. As the feed pressure increased, the stage cut changed at each stage, increasing from 0.20 to 0.30 at the 1 st stage and from 0.43 to 0.49 at the 2 nd stage and decreasing from 0.69 to 0.53 at the 3 rd stage. Moreover, increasing feed pressure increased both the permeate and retentate flow rates due to increased driving force of the 1st stage membrane module. As can be seen in Section 3.2, with increasing stage-cut, the flow rate of permeate increased more in the given membrane area. Therefore, the CH4 purity of the retentate side increased and the CO2 purity of the permeate side increased with the stage cut increase of the 1 st stage. This means that high-purity CO2 is supplied into the 3rd stage, and high-purity CH4 is supplied into the 2nd stage by the stage cut increase in the 1st stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 10(b) shows the operation results obtained for operating temperatures of 5, 17 and 24 °C. The purity and recovery of CH4 decreased from 99 to 96.5% and from 98.2 to 96.7, respectively, as the operation temperature increased from 5 to 24 °C at constant pressure. This behavior is explained by the CO2/CH4 selectivity data for the pure gas permeance of the 2-inch modules in Fig. (b). The permeances of CH4 and CO2 decreased with decreasing feed temperature, while the CO2/ CH4 selectivity increased. Therefore, the CO2/CH4 separation efficiency increased with lower operation temperature. The results of the mixed gas experiment in Fig. 7 supports that operation temperature can significantly affect the CH4 recovery and purity. It was confirmed that, using the 3-stage membrane process, a higher CH4 recovery ratio was obtained at a lower operation temperature.

Operation pressures of 9-10 bar and operation temperatures of about 5-15 °C were maintained. The obtained results are shown in Table 5. At a membrane area ratio of 1:1:1, with increasing total membrane area from 720 to 960 m 2 the CH4 purity increased from 96.1 to 98.4% and the recovery ratio decreased from 98.5 to 97.4%. At the same membrane area ratio of 1:1:1, the CO2 purity decreased from 96.9 to 95.1% and the recovery ratio decreased from 98.5 to 97.4%. A similar trend was also observed at a membrane area ratio of 1:2:1. This can be attributed to the fact that stage cut increases in the 1st and 3rd stages and decreases in the 2 nd stage due to increased membrane area. As the 1st stage membrane area increased, the high-purity CH4 is supplied to the 2 nd stage, and thus, the purity of CO2 decreased in the 3rd stage permeate. This means that a trade-off relationship is observed, i.e., when the membrane area increases, the purity of CH4 increased, its recovery ratio decreases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, from the obtained results, the optimum membrane area for treating 100 Nm 3 /h of biogas feed mixture was precisely determined, and the optimized 3rd stage membrane process achieved superior biogas upgrading performance. This included high CH4 purity above 98.4% at a high recovery ratio of 98.7% and high CO2 purity above 97.2% at a high recovery ratio of 97.0% from a crude biogas composition of about 70.9-74.2 vol% CH4/25.8-29.1 vol% CO2. This optimized process can be successfully applied to produce high purity methane automobile fuel (CH4 > 97%) and city gas fuel (CH4 > 97%) that meet biogas fuel standards; high purity carbon dioxide for carbon capture and utilization (CCU) is also produced.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A pilot-scale 3-stage membrane process with recirculation using one compressor was successfully designed, constructed, operated and used to optimize the production of high purity CH4 and CO2 with 100 Nm 3 /h of biogas mixture. For this, asymmetric polysulfone hollow fiber membrane modules and the COMPAQ Visual Fortran simulation program were used, as previously developed in our studies. Pure gas permeation and separation results against feed temperature and pressure matched well with the mixed gas results. It was found that CH4 concentration and recovery rates of gases were affected by changing feed temperatures and pressures and by mixed gas feed compositions. For the given feed conditions, CH4 purity and CH4 recovery in retentate were also significantly governed by increasing stage-cut.
texts.text: A pilot-scale 3-stage membrane process with recirculation using one compressor was successfully designed, constructed, operated and used to optimize the production of high purity CH4 and CO2 with 100 Nm 3 /h of biogas mixture. For this, asymmetric polysulfone hollow fiber membrane modules and the COMPAQ Visual Fortran simulation program were used, as previously developed in our studies. Pure gas permeation and separation results against feed temperature and pressure matched well with the mixed gas results. It was found that CH4 concentration and recovery rates of gases were affected by changing feed temperatures and pressures and by mixed gas feed compositions. For the given feed conditions, CH4 purity and CH4 recovery in retentate were also significantly governed by increasing stage-cut.

texts.orig: In the 100 Nm 3 /h scale pilot plant with about 70.9-74.2 vol% CH4/ 25.8-29.1 vol% CO2 feed, it was confirmed that effective biogas separation and purification can achieved at optimized operating pressures (9-10 bar) and temperatures (5-15 °C). When the membrane area ratios (1st:2nd:3rd) in the 3-stage process were varied from 1:1:1 to 1:3:1, the CH4 recovery ratio remained almost constant whereas the CH4 purity increased significantly. Increasing the total membrane area in the pilot plant also significantly increased the CH4 purity, from 96.1 to 98.4%, and decreased the recovery ratio from 98.5 to 97.4%. The effective membrane area was varied at fixed membrane area ratios of 1:1:1 and 1:2:1 and it was found that CH4 purity increased with increasing membrane area, revealing a trade-off relationship between recovery ratio and purity. As a result, it was confirmed that 2nd stage membrane area effects CH4 purity and recovery in retentate.

When the membrane area ratios (1st:2nd:3rd) in the 3-stage process were varied from 1:1:1 to 1:3:1, the CH4 recovery ratio remained almost constant whereas the CH4 purity increased significantly. Increasing the total membrane area in the pilot plant also significantly increased the CH4 purity, from 96.1 to 98.4%, and decreased the recovery ratio from 98.5 to 97.4%. The effective membrane area was varied at fixed membrane area ratios of 1:1:1 and 1:2:1 and it was found that CH4 purity increased with increasing membrane area, revealing a trade-off relationship between recovery ratio and purity. As a result, it was confirmed that 2nd stage membrane area effects CH4 purity and recovery in retentate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The optimized 3-stage membrane process achieved superior biogas upgrading performance, including high CH4 purity above 98.4 at a high recovery ratio of 98.7% and high CO2 purity above 97.2% at a high recovery ratio of 97.0% from crude biogas composition with 70.9-74.2 vol% CH4/25.8-29.1 vol% CO2. Based on these results, longterm stability of pilot plant operation and economic assessments including feasibility study are continuing in our laboratory.

Based on these results, longterm stability of pilot plant operation and economic assessments including feasibility study are continuing in our laboratory.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study suggests a basic framework to understand the effects of feed conditions on the separation behaviors of pure and mixed gas permeation properties, and their relationship with process condition of the multi-stage membrane process for biogas separation. This study also demonstrates that the 3-stage membrane process using asymmetric polysulfone hollow fiber membranes can be successfully used in the future for methane production for automotive fuel (CNG) and city gas applications, as well as for carbon dioxide recovery for carbon capture and utilization.
